behaviors.description: Speech-to-text API output preserves speaker labels so attendees can be attributed statements in the summary
behaviors.justification: Testing this requires calling the real speech-to-text API. With a mocked API, we'd never discover if the real API omits speaker labels, returns them in an unexpected format, or if our parser doesn't handle the actual response structure. Integration-level mocking would always return well-formed speaker labels, hiding format divergence and API contract issues. A real API call reveals whether speaker label extraction actually works end-to-end.
behaviors.test_description: Record a real meeting audio file (~10 min) with multiple speakers, send to real speech-to-text API, verify returned transcript contains speaker identifiers (e.g., 'Speaker 1', 'Speaker 2') and that attribution works correctly in downstream processing. Run at least 3 times to verify consistency.

behaviors.description: Structured summary contains at least one action item for every attendee who was assigned a task during the meeting
behaviors.justification: This is an extraction quality judgment that depends entirely on what the real LLM produces. A mocked LLM can be hardcoded to always return action items for all attendees, passing tests while the real LLM might miss attendees or fail to extract tasks. The LLM's ability to recognize 'task assignment' language variations and extract them reliably is non-deterministic across different meeting contexts. Only running the real LLM against diverse meeting transcripts (with ground truth) reveals whether this invariant actually holds.
behaviors.test_description: Create 5+ meeting transcripts with varying task-assignment language (e.g., 'Alice will handle...', 'We need Bob to...', 'Chris, can you deliver...?'). For each, call real LLM to produce summary. Verify: (1) every attendee assigned ≥1 task appears in action items, (2) no false positives (action items don't refer to non-attendees), (3) consistency—same transcript summarized twice produces action items for same attendees. Measure precision/recall against manually annotated ground truth.

behaviors.description: Key decisions mentioned in transcript appear in the 'decisions' section of the summary, not buried in narrative
behaviors.justification: This requires the real LLM to correctly categorize decisions into a structured 'decisions' section rather than leaving them in narrative text. A mock LLM can be configured to always place decisions in the right section, but the real LLM may conflate decisions with action items, narrative context, or simply fail to recognize decision language. The LLM's categorization of 'what is a key decision' is non-deterministic and domain-sensitive. Only testing against real LLM output reveals whether the summary structure accurately reflects actual decision detection.
behaviors.test_description: Use 5+ meeting transcripts containing explicit decisions (e.g., 'We decided to migrate to service X', 'The team agreed on Q3 timeline'). Call real LLM to summarize. Parse output JSON to verify: (1) each decision appears in 'decisions' section, not narrative, (2) narrative section does not duplicate decision text, (3) no false positives (non-decisions categorized as decisions). Compare LLM's extracted decisions against manually annotated ground truth for precision and recall.

behaviors.description: Audio files longer than 60 minutes are split into segments, processed sequentially, and merged into a single coherent summary
behaviors.justification: While splitting logic and sequential processing can be verified at Integration level, the critical failure mode is LLM-level: a merged transcript from multiple segments may confuse the LLM or lose context, producing an incoherent or contradictory summary. Testing with a mocked LLM would pass (mock always returns perfect output), but the real LLM might struggle with stitching segment boundaries, managing context between segments, or handling overlapping speakers across boundaries. The 'coherent summary' requirement specifically depends on real LLM judgment. System-level tests with mocked LLM would hide coherence failures.
behaviors.test_description: Create or obtain a real 75+ minute meeting recording. Verify service: (1) splits into ≤60 min segments, (2) processes segments in correct order with real speech-to-text API, (3) merges transcripts, (4) passes merged transcript to real LLM. Inspect final summary for coherence: no contradictions between segments, action items and decisions correctly attributed across segment boundaries, narrative flow intact. Run 3+ times to check consistency. Include a transcript with speaker transitions near segment boundaries to catch context loss.

behaviors.description: Full pipeline (upload audio, transcribe, summarize, deliver structured output) completes without manual intervention for a real meeting recording
behaviors.justification: This is an end-to-end test of the entire system with real infrastructure. It requires: (1) real file upload and storage handling, (2) real speech-to-text API call, (3) real LLM summarization, (4) structured output delivery. Testing at System level with mocked LLM would miss LLM failures. Testing with mocked APIs would miss actual integration issues (network timeout, malformed API responses, file permission issues). The behavior explicitly requires a 'real meeting recording'—a synthetic transcript won't catch audio handling failures. Only a full Workflow test with all real components reveals silent failures like partial uploads, API timeouts, malformed merges, or serialization issues.
behaviors.test_description: Select 3 diverse real meeting recordings (30 min, 60 min, 90 min durations; different audio quality/speaker accents). Upload each through the full API: audio upload → transcription → summarization → structured JSON output. Verify: (1) each stage completes without errors or timeouts, (2) output is valid JSON matching expected schema, (3) output contains non-empty decisions, action_items, attendees, and transcript sections, (4) audio metadata (duration, speaker count) is preserved. Monitor for silent failures: truncated uploads, malformed API responses, memory leaks during LLM calls. Record end-to-end latency for each file size.
